frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Sometimes I just think about Maa. No matter how busy or tired she was, our food was always ready, made with so much love and care. Not just food, but a blessing. She never asked for credit, just happily served everyone, truly putting our needs first. Today's fast food or even these fancy restaurants can't even come close to that warmth and true 'mamta'. That was real sacrifice, isn't it?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment glorifies the mother's tireless domestic labor, specifically cooking, as an act of pure love and sacrifice ('mamta'), implicitly erasing the labor aspect ('never asked for credit'). It sets an idealized, unattainable standard by comparing it unfavorably to modern food options, thereby subtly reinforcing the expectation that women's primary role is self-sacrificing domestic service, particularly in the kitchen.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 44. A regional manager at a pharma company in Ahmedabad. You are on a phone call with your old friend who runs his own business. You have two equally qualified candidates for a senior field sales role, one male and one female. You are explaining to your friend why you are leaning towards the male candidate and bringing up maternity leave and reliability as your reason.
